title: Moros y Cristianos
subtitle: Cuban black beans and rice cooked together in one pot
excerpt: Black beans and white rice cooked together in a single pot with a Cuban sofrito, so the rice absorbs the bean-infused liquid and turns a deep, speckled colour. A one-pot version of the Cuban staple.
sourceNotes: Moros y Cristianos, 'Moors and Christians', is the Cuban dish of black beans and white rice cooked together in the same pot, named for the contrast between the dark beans and the white rice. It differs from the separate frijoles negros and arroz blanco in that the rice cooks in bean-infused liquid and absorbs the colour and flavour. The Cuban sofrito base of green pepper, onion, garlic, cumin, and oregano gives the dish its character. It is effectively the Cuban version of rice and peas.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions. Reheat with a splash of water, covered, in a pan or microwave.

content.text: Moros y Cristianos is what happens when you cook the Cuban black beans and the rice together rather than separately. The rice absorbs the bean-infused liquid as it cooks and takes on a deep, mottled appearance — the 'Moors and Christians' of the name. The flavour is more complex than plain rice, with the earthy sweetness of the beans throughout each grain.

The key is managing the liquid ratio. The beans have already absorbed liquid and will release more as they cook with the rice; too much water and the rice ends up waterlogged and starchy. Reserve some of the bean liquid if using tinned — it is flavoured and worth using as part of the cooking liquid.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a heavy saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and {{pepper-green}} green pepper. Cook for 7 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{oregano-dried}} of oregano. Stir for 1 minute.

Add the {{black-beans}} of drained black beans (reserving their tin liquid). Add the {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and enough liquid to cover the rice by 2 cm: use the reserved bean liquid topped up with water to make about 550 ml total. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a boil, stirring once to combine. Reduce to the lowest possible simmer and cover tightly. Cook for 18 minutes without lifting the lid.

Remove from the heat and leave covered for 5 minutes. Lift the lid, remove the bay leaves, and fluff the rice gently with a fork. The rice should be deep purple-black from the beans, each grain separate.

items.cause: Too much liquid; lid opened during cooking
items.fix: Replace the lid and rest off the heat for 10 more minutes. The residual steam will finish the rice. Measure liquid carefully next time.

items.cause: Not rinsed before cooking; stirred during cooking
items.fix: Rinse rice in cold water until it runs clear before adding. Stir only once when adding the liquid, then don't touch it.
Add the tinned beans when the rice goes in, not earlier. They only need the 18-minute steam, not a pre-cook.

items.symptom: Pale colour — rice not taking the bean colour
items.cause: Too much water diluting the bean liquid
items.fix: Use as much bean liquid as possible from the tin, topped up with water only as needed. The bean starch in the liquid is what colours the rice.

content.text: Moros y Cristianos is as central to Cuban cooking as rice and peas is to Jamaican cooking — the two dishes are distant cousins, both built on the Caribbean combination of beans and rice but shaped by entirely different culinary traditions. The Cuban version is secular, everyday food eaten alongside ropa vieja, picadillo, or fried pork. The name dates to the period of Spanish colonial rule and refers to the colour contrast between the black beans and the white rice, though the two are cooked together until that contrast blurs into something more integrated.
